original_abstract: This research explores the perspective of Islamic family law regarding the rejection of marriage contracts for LGBT couples, analyzed through the conceptual framework of Maslahah (public interest). Utilizing a qualitative literature-based approach, the study emphasizes that marriage in Islam transcends a mere bilateral agreement; it is a sacred institution governed by specific Sharia pillars, including the requirement for male and female parties, a legal guardian (wali), a dowry (mahr), and credible witnesses. Because LGBT unions do not satisfy these fundamental legal requirements, they are not recognized as valid marriage contracts within the construction of Islamic family law. Furthermore, the study applies Maslahah theory to interpret this rejection not as an act of arbitrary discrimination, but as a strategic effort to preserve the integrity of the family structure, uphold traditional moral values, and ensure social and spiritual harmony. By prioritizing the collective welfare and the preservation of the family as the primary unit of society, Islamic law functions as a dynamic system that seeks to balance religious mandates with the pursuit of justice. Ultimately, the research concludes that the refusal to recognize LGBT marriage contracts is rooted in a deep-seated commitment to maintaining societal stability and religious continuity while navigating the challenges of contemporary social shifts.
humanized_abstract: This paper seeks to examine the stance of the Islamic family law towards the rejection of the marriage contract of LGBT individuals. This is explored using Maslahah (public interest) as an analytical framework. By adopting a qualitative literature-based approach, it was realised that according to the Islamic family law, the marriage contract is a contract that exceeds being merely a mutual agreement that binds two parties by the Sharia pillars as prescribed by Islam. These are usually fulfilled with presence of men and women who intend to get married (i.e. the husband and wife) whom a wali (or its representation) is appointed and also the mahr (or dowers) promised and ascertained in presence of two witnesses whose testimony is admissible in court. Subsequently the LGBTs’ unions or agreements are not considered legitimate marriage contracts. The Maslahah concept helps the Islamic scholars in interpreting the legal position of the dismissal of the marriage contract of the LGBT. Instead of being treated as a form of discrimination, the stance is seen as a measure that maintains social values such as that of the sanctity of the family structure of the family institution. Thus Islam sought in itself to combine the rules and requirements with justice. Through applying the principle of Maslahah (Public interest) which represents a juristic school of thought concerning justice, this paper realises the motivation and causes behind their rejection to grant recognition to the marriage contracts to the LGBTs. Their intention for nonrecognition towards LGBT marriages was derived from their strong effort in protecting the social values, religious system as well as controlling the arising conflicts because of the changing contemporary society.
